entry.title: Handling Concurrent Updates in CAP Java
entry.content:
The Problem: Lost Updates

Consider two users, Alice and Bob, accessing the same employee record simultaneously.

Alice updates the employee’s email address and saves the record.
Shortly afterward, Bob updates the employee’s last name and saves his changes.

However, Bob’s update was based on an earlier version of the record, prior to Alice’s modification. As a result, his save operation unintentionally overwrites Alice’s update without any conflict detection or warning.

This scenario is known as a lost update - a common concurrency issue in multi-user systems where one user’s changes silently overwrite another’s, leading to data integrity problems.


Why Not Just Lock the Record?

One way to prevent lost updates is pessimistic locking. As soon as Alice opens the record, the system locks it, preventing others from modifying it until she finishes.

While effective in theory, this approach is poorly suited for web applications. Users may leave forms open for extended periods, causing records to remain locked unnecessarily and blocking other users from making legitimate updates.

Under real-world traffic, long-held locks can quickly become a source of contention, reduced concurrency, and poor user experience.

The Solution: Optimistic Locking

Optimistic locking takes a different approach to concurrency control. Instead of locking records when they are read, the system allows multiple users to access and modify data concurrently.

The validation happens at save time. Before applying an update, the system checks whether the record has changed since the user originally retrieved it.

  • If the record has been modified in the meantime, the update is rejected to prevent overwriting newer data.
  • If the record is unchanged, the update succeeds and the record’s version is advanced.

This approach avoids long-held locks while still protecting the application from lost updates and unintended data overwrites.

How It Works: ETags

Optimistic locking on the web is implemented using ETags, a standard HTTP mechanism.

When a record is retrieved, the server includes an ETag in the response. This acts as a version identifier that changes whenever the record is updated.

When sending an update, the client includes this ETag in the If-Match header, effectively stating: apply this update only if the record is still on the same version I read.

The server then compares the provided ETag with the current version:

  • Match → The record is unchanged, the update is applied, and a new ETag is generated
  • Mismatch → The record has been modified by someone else, and the request is rejected with 412 Precondition Failed

This ensures updates are applied only to the latest version of the data, preventing silent overwrites.

How CAP Java Does It

CAP Java handles all of this automatically. We just need to tell the framework which field to use as the ETag.

We can do that with a single annotation in our CDS model:


The managed aspect automatically maintains the modifiedAt timestamp, updating it whenever the record is changed. By annotating this field with @odata.etag, CAP uses it as the entity’s version identifier (ETag).

From that point onward, concurrency control is handled automatically. For every update request, CAP validates the incoming If-Match header against the current value of modifiedAt. If the values differ, the framework rejects the update with an HTTP 412 Precondition Failed response, preventing stale data from overwriting newer changes.

No additional Java code is required.

Seeing It in Action

Let us start the application and create an employee record:


As we can see the response contains the ETag value: 2026-05-25T06:55:44.400909Z

Let us simulate Alice’s request of updating the email with the ETag we just received:


This succeeds. The record is updated and a new ETag is generated.

Now, simulating Bob's request (who is working with the older version) using the older ETag value.


Bob's request is rejected with 412 Precondition Failed.
Alice's change is safe.

What Happens Next: Resolving the Conflict

A 412 Precondition Failed response is not an error to suppress - it is a signal that the data has changed since it was last read.
At this point, the user can reload the latest version of the data, review the differences, and reapply their changes with full context before saving again.

Hence, Bob retrieves the latest version of the record and obtains the updated ETag value.


Bob resubmits his update using the fresh ETag:  2026-05-25T07:07:15.691711Z



This time it succeeds. Both changes are now in the record. Nothing was lost.

The key insight is that a 412 is not a failure - it is a safety net doing its job. The system caught a potential lost update before it happened and gave Bob the opportunity to resolve it cleanly.

Conclusion

Concurrency-related data integrity issues are particularly problematic because they occur silently. When two users update the same record, the later save succeeds and the earlier change is overwritten without any indication of a conflict.

Optimistic locking is the standard approach to preventing this class of issue, and CAP Java provides built-in support. By adding a simple @odata.etag annotation, the framework can automatically detect stale updates and reject conflicting changes before any data is lost.

It is a minimal change to the data model, but one that significantly improves the consistency and reliability of enterprise applications.

entry.title: E-Invoicing for self billing in France E-Invoice
entry.content: We are implementing SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) for France E‑Invoicing. As part of the mandate, we are required to use self‑invoicing; however, SAP currently does not provide a standard solution for this scenario. According to French compliance requirements, self‑invoices must be generated by us through MRRL and subsequently transmitted to the approved Plateformes de Dématérialisation Partenaires (PAs). We kindly request your guidance and a proposed solution to address this gap, as this is critical for ensuring compliance with the French e‑invoicing law. Thank you in advance for your support and prompt attention to this matter.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026: The Autonomous Enterprise Has Arrived
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire 2026: The Autonomous Enterprise Has Arrived

A complete summary of the biggest AI announcements from Orlando, May 13–14, 2026

50+

Joule Assistants

200+

Specialized Agents

€100M

Partner AI Fund

26

Industries

SAP Sapphire 2026 in Orlando marked what many are calling the most significant shift in enterprise software in decades. CEO Christian Klein unveiled the Autonomous Enterprise — a bold repositioning of SAP from 'software that records what people do' to 'software that does the work itself.' With over 50 Joule Assistants and 200+ specialized AI agents shipping across Finance, Spend Management, Supply Chain, HR, and Customer Experience, SAP is making a clear statement: the era of autonomous business operations is here.

This blog post covers all 12 major announcement areas from the event, based on official SAP News Center announcements.

1. The Autonomous Enterprise — Christian Klein's Vision

"Eighty percent accuracy isn't sufficient when running mission-critical businesses. AI must work at enterprise grade — accurate, compliant, and secure."  — Christian Klein, CEO, SAP

The keynote set the tone for the entire conference. SAP's strategic pivot centers on three pillars:

  • SAP Business AI Platform — A unified build-and-govern layer combining BTP, Business Data Cloud, and AI Foundation with SAP Knowledge Graph at its core
  • SAP Autonomous Suite — 200+ specialized agents and 50+ Joule Assistants across all major business functions, shipping now
  • Joule Work — A completely new human interface replacing traditional app navigation, described as the most significant UX shift since Fiori

A live on-stage demo with H&M showcased Store Intelligence Agents and an AI-powered InStore Concierge delivering personalized product recommendations — a compelling real-world proof point of the vision.

2. SAP Business AI Platform — One Foundation

BTP, Business Data Cloud, and AI Foundation have been unified into a single architecture, with the SAP Knowledge Graph at the core:

01

SAP Knowledge Graph: 452,000 SAP tables encoded; 7.3 million data fields with semantic relationships; 50 years of SAP ERP engineering

02

Anthropic Claude: Named primary AI reasoning model powering Joule — multi-step reasoning for enterprise-grade workflows with full security and compliance controls

03

Joule Studio 2.0 (GA Now): AI-first dev environment integrating n8n visual workflow, Vercel design tooling, and NVIDIA OpenShell secure runtime

04

SAP AI Agent Hub (Q3 2026): Central marketplace for discovering, managing, and governing AI agents — 680+ partner submissions already received

05

Prior Labs / SAP-RPT-1.5: Tops TabArena benchmark — 3.5x better than LLMs on structured enterprise predictions

06

Dremio (Q3 2026): Open Lakehouse acquisition extending Business Data Cloud with open-format analytics at scale

07

Microsoft Fabric Connect (GA): SAP data flows natively into Microsoft Fabric without duplication — zero-copy integration

08

AWS Zero-Copy (GA): SAP data accessible in Amazon Athena — no data movement required

3. Joule Work — The New Human Interface

Joule Work abandons traditional app navigation entirely. Describe outcomes in natural language and Joule coordinates data, workflows, and agents to execute. This is the biggest UX shift since Fiori.

Availability

  • Mobile App — Available Now: Full Joule Work experience on mobile devices
  • Web Client — Early Adopter Care: GA targeted for H2 2026
  • Desktop App — Planned Q2 2026: Bridges SAP with local files, email, and documents
  • Voice via LiveKit — Early Adopter Care: Real-time voice AI for field, factory, and warehouse

Interoperability Protocols

  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) — Available Now: Joule connects natively to Microsoft Copilot and third-party agents
  • Agent-to-Agent (A2A) Protocol — GA Q4 2026: Bidirectional integration with Salesforce Agentforce and ServiceNow

4. Autonomous Finance — 7 Joule Assistants (GA Q3 2026)

"72% of organizations find financial planning too time-consuming."  — SAP Research

Finance operations follow a Sense–Reason–Act model: detect signals, evaluate constraints in real time, connect plans to execution — automatically. The 7 Joule Assistants cover the full financial lifecycle:

01

Financial Closing Assistant: Compresses close cycle from weeks to days

02

Financial Planning Assistant: Continuous planning with SAP Analytics Cloud

03

Billing Assistant: Detects inconsistencies before invoice generation

04

Governance Assistant: CSRD and regulatory compliance tracking

05

Tax & Compliance Assistant: BEPS Pillar 2 and global e-invoicing mandates

06

Accounts Receivable Assistant: Churn prediction and automated dunning

07

Cash & Treasury Assistant: Real-time liquidity visibility and allocation

SAP Enterprise Planning is the new flagship product — moving finance from periodic planning cycles to continuous steering, with Joule Agents embedded to monitor signals and recommend actions automatically.

5. Autonomous Spend Management — 11 Joule Assistants

The full source-to-pay lifecycle is now covered by 11 Joule Assistants, addressing long-standing fragmentation between procurement, finance, and operations. Key milestones include Ariba Buying reaching GA and Fieldglass introducing AI-enhanced contractor management. Assistants span Category Management, Sourcing, Contract Management, Supplier Collaboration, Purchase Requisitions, Invoice Processing, Expense Management, Travel Management, Workforce Planning, Contingent Workforce, and Spend Analytics.

6. Autonomous Supply Chain — 6 Joule Assistants + 60+ Agents

Supply chain sees 6 Joule Assistants alongside 60+ specialized agents covering demand planning, production scheduling, logistics, and asset management. Notable highlights:

  • NVIDIA Partnership — Factory-edge AI for sub-second quality inspection and anomaly detection
  • RWE Demo — Offshore wind turbine maintenance using autonomous asset management agents
  • Assistants cover: Demand Planning, Production Scheduling, Logistics, Inventory Management, Supplier Risk, and Asset Maintenance

7. Autonomous HCM — 13 Joule Assistants (All GA June 2026)

Human Capital Management gets the largest single-function rollout — 13 Joule Assistants all reaching General Availability in June 2026. The suite covers the complete employee lifecycle from recruiting through retirement, with notable integrations:

  • SmartRecruiters Integration — AI-enhanced talent acquisition workflows
  • Workforce Planning — Predictive headcount and skills gap analysis
  • Full coverage: Recruiting, Onboarding, Learning, Performance, Succession, Compensation, Payroll, Benefits, Time & Attendance, Employee Experience, HR Service Delivery, Analytics, and Workforce Planning

8. Autonomous Customer Experience — 11 Joule Assistants

The CX suite is built on the principle that every customer interaction reflects how well the business runs — AI must operate across the full execution chain. 11 Joule Assistants cover marketing through service:

  • Content, Campaign, Merchandising, Shopping, Order Management
  • Sales, Deal Qualification, Deal Closing
  • Case Management, Service Management, Content Generation

Key CX Updates

  • SAP Commerce Cloud for Mid-Market — GA Q3 2026
  • Composable Commerce Services — New modular cart and checkout
  • Google Cloud — Gemini Enterprise + Universal Commerce Protocol for multimodal personalized journeys
  • Parloa — Voice and digital self-service in SAP Service Cloud
  • Adyen / Checkout.com / PayPal — Embedded payment processing in SAP Commerce workflows

9. Migration & Modernisation — 35%+ Effort Reduction

 

35%+

ERP Migration Effort Reduction

3

Joule Assistants in RISE Year 1

3

S/4HANA Release Cycles Certified

 

  • RISE with SAP Restructured — Now activates 3 Joule Assistants contractually in Year 1; ECC customers can access select AI scenarios immediately before full migration completes
  • SAP GROW — Full Joule portfolio access from day one for mid-market cloud-first customers
  • Clean Core Certification — Formally certifies BTP extensions as upgrade-compatible across three S/4HANA Cloud release cycles
  • Palantir AIP Partnership (GA Q3 2026) — SAP Endorsed App status with Accenture as global strategic services partner

 

10. Strategic Partnerships Reshaping the Ecosystem

SAP announced 11 strategic alliances spanning AI reasoning, infrastructure, data, experience, and interoperability:

 

01

Anthropic: Claude named primary reasoning model powering Joule across the full Autonomous Suite

02

NVIDIA: OpenShell runtime for Joule Studio; factory-edge inference for sub-second quality inspection

03

Microsoft: Fabric Connect GA; A2A interoperability with Microsoft Copilot (Q4 2026)

04

Google Cloud: Gemini Enterprise for CX; Universal Commerce Protocol; Merchant Center integration

05

AWS: Zero-copy SAP data access via Amazon Athena — no data movement required

06

Palantir: AIP as SAP Endorsed App + Solution Extension; GA Q3 2026 with Accenture as global partner

07

Accenture: Global strategic services partner for Palantir AIP and complex S/4 migrations

08

n8n: Visual workflow builder integrated into Joule Studio for no-code agent design

09

Vercel: Design tooling in Joule Studio; accelerates storefront development for CX

10

LiveKit: Real-time voice AI for Joule Work in field, factory, and warehouse settings

11

Parloa: AI agents in SAP Service Cloud for end-to-end voice and digital service resolution

 

11. Partner Ecosystem — €100M Business AI Fund

"A Symphony of Partnership to Ring in the Era of the Autonomous Enterprise."  — Karl Fahrbach, Chief Partner Officer, SAP

The €100M Business AI Partner-Led Adoption Program is structured around four service packages:

  • Adoption — Entry level: deploy existing AI assistants with structured enablement
  • Launch — Build and deploy at least one custom agent via Joule Studio
  • Performance — Demonstrate measurable business outcomes from deployed agents
  • Enterprise — Comprehensive AI strategy delivery with full Joule Studio mastery

 

Additional milestones include a new Validated Partner Designation for AI and data strategy expertise, the Partner Agent Race to SAP TechEd with 680+ submissions, and a full enablement curriculum across sales, presales, consulting, and development roles.

 

12. Industry AI — 26 Sectors at GA

SAP is embedding domain logic directly into the Autonomous Suite for 26 industries. Seven sectors are live at launch:

  • Utilities — Autonomous asset management; RWE offshore wind turbine maintenance demo
  • Manufacturing — Factory-edge AI with NVIDIA; quality inspection and production excellence agents
  • Life Sciences — Compliance-aware manufacturing and regulatory documentation for pharma
  • Consumer & Retail — Unified commerce, revenue growth management, AI-powered in-store concierge (H&M live keynote)
  • Oil, Gas & Chemicals — Asset-intensive scenarios via Fieldglass; contractor lifecycle and credential verification
  • Engineering & Construction — Project and resource management with supply chain integration
  • + 20 More Industries — Agriculture, Banking, Insurance, and 17 others at full GA

 

Key Takeaways for the SAP Community

SAP Sapphire 2026 wasn't a roadmap event — it was a delivery event. The capabilities announced are shipping now or within the next two quarters. Here's what the SAP community needs to know:

 

  1. Joule Work changes how users interact with SAP

The shift from menu-driven navigation to natural language is fundamental. Partners and customers should begin planning adoption strategies now.

  1. Clean Core is now certifiable and incentivized

With Clean Core Certification and the RISE restructuring, there's never been a clearer path for ECC customers to modernize without losing access to AI capabilities during migration.

  1. The partner opportunity is enormous

The €100M fund, 680+ agent submissions, and four-tier adoption program create a massive opportunity for SAP partners to build, deploy, and monetize AI capabilities.

  1. Industry AI is real and customer-validated

H&M and RWE weren't just demos — they were production-grade live deployments on stage. This signals that enterprise-grade autonomous operations are already in production for SAP's largest customers.

Based on official SAP News Center announcements from SAP Sapphire 2026, Orlando, May 13–14, 2026.

For more information, visit community.sap.com

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: SAP certified - Security administrator
entry.content:
Security and user access management are often behind the scenes.  Until something breaks.  Then everyone notices.

That’s why strong SAP security expertise matters more than ever across SAP S/4HANA and cloud environments.

Our upcoming “Partner Learning Accelerator: SAP Certified – Security Administrator” is designed to help SAP Partners prepare for the C_SEC certification through a focused 6-hour live session.  We’ll cover the topics partners deal with every day: authorization concepts, identity and access management, business role design, SAP Fiori authorizations, plus troubleshooting access issues across SAP systems.

The thing is, certification prep can feel overwhelming when you’re balancing customer projects and deadlines.  This session helps cut through the noise and focus on the exam domains that matter most.

If you already work with SAP system administration or user management, this is a great next step to sharpen your expertise and validate your skills.

Register now and join us.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jun/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010506.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #SAPSecurity #SAPS4HANA #IdentityAccessManagement #SAPLearning

entry.title: Autonomous Enterprise: SAP змінює правила гри — і допомагає вам підготуватися
entry.content:
На конференції SAP Sapphire 2026 компанія SAP офіційно оголосила про старт концепції Autonomous Enterprise (Автономне підприємство)— фундаментальну зміну того, як проектуються, управляються та функціонують компанії в епоху ШІ.

З чого складається Autonomous Enterprise?

  • SAP Business AI Platform об’єднує BTP, SAP Business Data Cloud та SAP Business AI в єдине середовище. В основі — SAP Knowledge Graph та Joule Studio для створення агентів у форматі no-code, pro-code або AI-фреймворків.
  • SAP Autonomous Suite включає 50+ асистентів Joule та 200+ агентів для автоматизації наскрізних процесів. Наприклад, Autonomous Close Assistant може скоротити фінансове закриття з кількох тижнів до кількох днів.
  • Joule Work — єдина точка взаємодії замість десятків застосунків. Користувач описує бажаний результат, а Joule координує процеси, дані та агентів. Доступно на десктопі, мобільних пристроях та через голосовий інтерфейс.

Інвестиція у 100 млн євро

SAP запускає фонд для прискорення переходу до Autonomous Enterprise:

  • Клієнти RISE with SAP — три активовані асистенти Joule в перший рік
  • Клієнти SAP GROW — повний доступ до портфоліо з першого дня
  • Нові інструменти міграції скорочують зусилля на перехід до хмарного ERP більш ніж на 35%

Безкоштовна серія вебінарів

На тлі оголошень із Sapphire SAP запускає безкоштовну серію вебінарів «Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise» — 11 сесій, розроблених архітекторами для архітекторів.

📅 1 червня — 12 серпня 2026, щотижня
🕔 18:00–19:00 за українським часом

Кожна сесія ведеться практиками, лідерами продуктів, клієнтами та запрошеними спікерами, які самі будують, управляють або консультують щодо тих самих архітектурних рішень. Формат — одна сфокусована тема на сесію з повноцінним Q&A.

Програма сесій:

Тема

Дата

Unpacking SAP’s Autonomous Enterprise for Architects

1 червня 2026

What SAP’s Evolved Portfolio Means for Architects

10 червня 2026

Architecting From Intent to Action with Joule Work & Knowledge Graph

16 червня 2026

Playbook for Building Your Own SAP Agents (Hands-On)

26 червня 2026

SAP’s Innovation Factory for Architecting and Activating AI

1 липня 2026

AI Agent Lifecycle Management at Enterprise Scale

8 липня 2026

An Architect’s Guide to the SAP Family of Models

14 липня 2026

Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise

22 липня 2026

From Autonomous Domains to Industry AI: One Platform, Two Lenses

29 липня 2026

AI Governance and Security for an Autonomous Enterprise

5 серпня 2026

Role of an Enterprise Architect in Driving the Autonomous Enterprise

12 серпня 2026

* To be Finalised 
детальна інформація про спікерів та розклад - за посиланням

Для кого ця серія?
Для Enterprise, Solution, Data та AI Architects, Technology Leaders, а також SAP клієнтів і партнерів, що впроваджують або масштабують ШІ в корпоративних середовищах.

Що ви отримаєте?

  • Цілісну архітектурну модель Joule Work, SAP Autonomous Suite, Business AI Platform та Knowledge Graph
  •  Практичне керівництво з побудови та управління SAP-агентами
  •  Реальні кейси від Bayer та Henkel
  • Розуміння того, як роль Enterprise Architect еволюціонує в епоху ШІ

👉 Зареєструватися безкоштовно

Чому це важливо для українського бізнесу?

Autonomous Enterprise відкриває новий рівень конкурентоспроможності: скорочення операційних витрат, прискорення прийняття рішень та відповідність євроінтеграційним стандартам. Українські компанії мають унікальну можливість підготуватися до наступного етапу цифрової трансформації — вже зараз і безкоштовно.

Ера автономного підприємства вже настала. SAP дає всі інструменти, щоб бути серед перших.

I can't find examples anywhere (including SAP's link to the API document) to populate the Item -> Cost Distribution to a Sales Order / Line Number. The attached image shows where i do this and can see it in the Client however, where READing the Credit Memo (also attached with some redaction to comapny info etc) in PostMan, there's no hint of the reference to the Sales Order / Item.

My friend (CoPilot!) suggested it's potentially adding something along the lines of the below to the API POST however, although the POST did not return any errors, I didn't subsequently see anything on the resultant Credit Memo in the client on the Sales Order / Line item section...

I have also attached what i'm POSTing for reference

Any help here please ? (would be hugely appreciated!)

entry.title: Re: Mass change in the Purchase Scheduling Agreement Sche...
entry.content:
Have you already considered 

Fiori

  • Mass Changes to Purchase Scheduling Agreements - F2668

entry.title: Re: Scale your Applications using Custom Metrics Feature of Application Autoscaler
entry.content:
Hi @arsalankhan2021 

We are evaluating autoscaling metrics for our CAP Node.js application on Cloud Foundry.

Could you help clarify which metric would be more appropriate for our workload between memoryutil, cpu, and cpuutil?

From our understanding:

memoryutil reflects RAM consumption relative to the assigned memory quota
cpuutil reflects CPU entitlement utilization

Since our application involves files processing(TXT,DOCX,OCR,PDF) processing and AI orchestration workloads , we would like to understand:

which metric is generally recommended for scaling decisions in such scenarios
whether scaling should primarily be driven by memory pressure or CPU utilization

Also, could you explain in practical terms how memoryutil differs from cpuutil in Cloud Foundry environments, and why cpuutil is typically recommended?

Thanks
Kanika

entry.title: Re: Cash Flow Report Direct Method in S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @sanne_larsen 

Thank you for your question.

The Generic Cash Flow Statement requires a valid cashflow statement version ID. The error message means that the value maintained in Setting Up Your Statutory Reporting ID 101071 is either missing or does not match a valid cashflow statement version. See KBA 3449130 for resolution details.

However, if Define Cash Flow Statement Version for Several Countries ID 105331 is not available in your system, then you cannot maintain the required cash flow statement version through that activity. Since the configuration activity is marked as Specialised for Certain Countries/Regions, it is only delivered for specific localised country or region scenarios. That means the limitation is not that J78 is inactive but that the required configuration activity is not available for the countries in your current scope.

For SE, DK, BE, NL and ES, where the cashflow statement is not a legal statutory requirement in your scenario, the standard DRC statutory report route may therefore not be the correct basis for an internal management cash flow report.

What I suggest you do instead is as follows:

  • Option 1, because you have the exact error mentioned in the KBA earlier and the required SSCUI is not available, i suggest you raise an incident and ask whether Generic Cash Flow Statement with SCCUI 105331 is supported for your scoped countries and release.
  • Option 2, if the requirement is internal management reporting and not legal reporting, consider using an analytical report approach instead of Run Statutory Reports.                                                                                                               

Depending on your reporting needs, the alternatives you have are:

  • Use standard cash management and liquidity reporting apps if your requirement is operational cash flow visibility.
  • Use financial statement/journal entry analytical reporting if the requirement is a structured finance report.
  • Build a custom analytical report based on journal entry line items and cash relevant account logic.
  • Use SAP Analytics Cloud or external reporting if you need a management format across multiple countries.

Note that DRC statutory reporting framework is normally best suited for legally defined country reports. If the countries do not require a statutory cash flow statement and the configuration activity for them is not delivered, using DRC as the base may not be the right approach.

 

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4de200b26d5d42bbacae69c6bf6b9923/331dd0bd74fe480087e1deeec032aea0.html

 

  • Option 3 is for you to consider submitting an improvement request. See KBA 2963059 for more details.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Exploring the App “Origin of Goods” in SAP GTS
entry.content:
As an importer or a domestic operator, accurately collecting and documenting the origin of the externally-procured product and batches is of the utmost importance since it determines the duties, possible trade preference options available, and import restrictions that apply to the shipment.

As a matter of fact, the Country/Region of Origin is a mandatory attribute for Customs treatment, duty assessment, and anti-dumping.

Non-preferential origin refers to the country where a product is either wholly obtained or entirely produced. It can also be the last country where the product underwent substantial transformation. Minor or minimal processing carried out in another location does not usually change the product’s origin status.

With the Manage Country/Region of Origin app, you can manage the country and region of origin for your externally procured products directly in SAP GTS.

Let us explore how this new feature works in SAP GTS Edition for Hana.

How does the flow of information look like?

  • Since there is no batch information in the Import declaration, the link is retrieved from inbound delivery item.
  • Both Import declaration before and after GR are supported.
  • Inbound Delivery can be transferred to GTS even if Customs Management is not activated consuming a certain API.

How does the back-end configuration look like?

Activate the plant for Origin of Goods.

Origin can be determined based on three sources, namely LTSD, Import declaration and Inbound Delivery. Based on the order of preference, the sequence can be configured.

The three sources of information.

1. Long Term Supplier Declaration

2. Customs Import Declaration

3. Inbound Delivery

How does the determination work?

Once the source documents have been transferred from S/4 to GTS, the determination of Country/Region of Origin report should be executed or scheduled as a background job to create new entries or updates in Manage Country/Region of Origin App.

Similarly, update of Batch Stock can be executed or scheduled as a background job to capture batches with stock in the determination base.

Working with “Manage Country/Region of Origin” app.

Upon executing, determination result list is populated for products and/or batches.

Exploring further down for a particular product, in determination base, the relevant list of material documents is also listed. This is the result of the report “determination of country/region of origin”

The logic is influenced by the “Determination Method” selection in the customizing.

In case of batch managed products, report “Schedule update of Batch Stock” constantly updates the current quantity. When the stock becomes zero, the batch is not relevant anymore for determining the Country or Region and will be moved to “Completed Batches”

When multiple batches are used without any unique value and alert is raised and the Country of origin is left blank at the header.

If the last batch listed in the determination base is relevant and has stock, then it is also possible to change in edit mode and mark it as expended. A user justification will be needed in such cases. Where upon the alert will be removed and Country of Origin at header level will become RO.

Important aspects to consider.

  1. In case a GR falls within the validity of a released Inbound LTSD, but CoO hasn't been maintained in the LTSD, the LTSD shouldn't be displayed in the app. Same applies for Inbound Delivery.
  2. If Inbound LTSD is created and released and has CoO maintained, but the Product is in Edit Mode, the new value will not be populated immediately. A background job can be scheduled to rerun.
  3. A reissued LTSD containing CoO, overwrites the old LTSD as a source.
  4. For the LTSDs where an alternative LTSD issuer exist than the actual supplier from the GR, there will be no updates with the LTSD document in the app.
  5. If an processed GR in the Origin of Goods App is cancelled in the Feeder System, that GR will be moved to Completed Documents after execution of “Schedule Determination of Country/Region”.
  6. In case of batches, if a GR is marked as expended, the GR is moved to completed documents, but also the corresponding batch is moved to completed batches.
  7. Source Overview will be populated as long the source document contains origin information. If no source exist for a particular GR, or the source documents do not contain origin, or the source is a manual maintenance of an user, the Source Overview will be empty.

Finally, once a unique country/region of origin is populated at the header level of the app, it is then automatically transferred back to the product master data in S4.

Prerequisites

  • Private Cloud S/4HANA 2021 and higher.
  • GTS Edition for Hana 2025, SP1

Application Help:

Origin of Goods | SAP Help Portal

Configuration Guide

Business Scenarios in Origin of Goods | SAP Help Portal

Administrator Guide

Origin of Goods | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: SAP Results Analysis - variance release based on good...
entry.content:
In my opinion you have two options:

- use period costing for production orders. Here you get WIP at target and variances for the period. Here you get WIP at target and variances for the period.

SAP: Product Cost by Order versus Product Cost by Period | SAP Help Portal

- activate WIP at actual prices in ML actual costing. This takes place both in Product Cost by Period and in Product Cost by Order.

You can even use both functionalities because they are not mutually exclusive. or just one of them. From the point of view of production that is already running, the second option may be easier. Both work, I have already used them on projects.

entry.title: Seamless Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On with the Joule Work mobile app
entry.content:
With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app (formerly known as SAP Mobile Start) introduces a feature that many customers have been asking for: seamless Single Sign-On when opening web applications in the in-app browser. In this post I want to explain the problem this feature solves, how it works conceptually, and what administrators need to configure to enable it.

The Problem: Two Sessions, One App Experience

Joule Work mobile app (formerly known as SAP Mobile Start), when connected to SAP Build Work Zone, is the native mobile entry point for the Intelligent Enterprise. From a single app, users can access native features like widgets, push notifications, and smartwatch complications, but also launch web applications directly from the app – for example SAP Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA, collaborative Workspaces in SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition, custom extensions build on the SAP Business Technology Platform, and more.

When a user taps on a tile that opens a web application, the Joule Work mobile app instantiates the in-app browser provided by the mobile operating system – iOS or Android. This is a technical context switch: the native application session, which is managed by SAP Mobile Services and backed by SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication, is logically separated from the browser sessions of web applications, for example SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition.

As a result, the in-app browser may not have a valid session for the target web application when launched. The user is redirected to the identity provider and prompted to log in again. This is particularly jarring because, from the user's perspective, they are just navigating within their app.

The Previous Recommendation – and Why It Was Not Ideal for all customers

Customers could avoid this by enabling the "Remember me" functionality of SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. This feature creates a long-living session cookie in the browser, keeping users signed in across browser sessions for a configurable duration. A similar capability exists on many corporate identity providers (third-party IdPs) to which SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication may be configured delegating the actual authentication. Long-lived cookies on these providers effectively keep the user session alive in the in-app browser in the same manner.

However, this approach has a fundamental drawback: long-lived session cookies are not ideal and may not be wanted to be generically configured on the company's Identity Provider. Furthermore, "Remember me" only prolongs the time until a user is asked to sign-in again in the in-app browser – it is not fully getting rid of it.

Another alternative that some customers consider is password-less sign-in using client certificates. While this is a genuinely secure and, once set up, a convenient approach, it is not feasible for all customers and all users. Client certificates typically require an active Mobile Device Management (MDM) system to push certificates to managed devices – meaning unmanaged devices, common in bring your own device (BYOD) environments, are excluded. Beyond the deployment challenge, mobile operating systems restrict access to the system keychain for third-party apps, so not all in-app browser variants can reliably make use of client certificates. Other password-less options such as FIDO passkeys or biometric-based flows can require some form of manual user interaction during authentication, which breaks the seamless experience users expect when tapping a tile in the app.

The New Solution: Mobile-to-Web SSO

With version 3.0, Joule Work mobile app introduces a dedicated Mobile-to-Web Single Sign-On flow that bridges the native session and the in-app browser – securely, without requiring long-lived cookies. Here is how it works conceptually:

The mobile app already holds a valid session with SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (in the context of SAP Build Work Zone, to which the app is connected to). The SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant generally serves as the trust anchor for both SAP Build Work Zone, the native app and other SAP and non-SAP solutions including web applications exposed to SAP Build Work Zone. This shared trust is what makes it possible to bridge the two worlds: because both the native app session and the target web applications are anchored in the same SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant, it can act as a secure intermediary, translating the native session into a web session in the in-app browser without requiring the user to authenticate again.

When the user opens a web application in the in-app browser, the app first exchanges its active native session for a short-lived, one-time use SSO token issued by SAP Cloud Identity Services. This token is valid for only a very short time and can only be used once by the mobile app for (re-)establishing a session in the in-app browser on the mobile device.

This is done by passing this one-time SSO token to SAP Cloud Identity Services within the browser context. The SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the token, maps it to the user's identity & native session, and establishes a regular web session – setting the standard browser session cookies. From there, the accessed target web application authenticates against SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication transparently via its standard SAML or OIDC flow reusing the established IAS session with no user interaction required.

The key properties of this approach:

  • Security-friendly: The SSO token is a one-time use token with a short validity. There are no long-lived session cookies involved on the identity provider side.
  • Admin-controlled: The feature can be controlled via a feature flag in SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit of the Joule Work mobile app. The administrator also defines exactly which web applications are trusted for this flow by configuring them in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.
  • Broadly compatible: The feature works with all in-app browser variants offered by Joule Work mobile app on iOS and Android. It also works with all web applications – SAP solutions as well as custom or third-party apps – as long as they share a common SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication tenant with the environment the mobile app is onboarded to.

Key Prerequisite: SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as the Common Trust Anchor

For the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow to work, SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication must be the identity provider for both the native mobile application and all web applications that should benefit from seamless SSO. The trust is established and maintained through SAP Cloud Identity Services, making it the single common denominator across all participants.

It is important to clarify that SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication does not need to be your end users' primary authentication system. In many enterprise landscapes, SAP Cloud Identity Services acts as an identity proxy, delegating the actual authentication to a corporate identity provider such as Microsoft Entra ID or other identity providers. This is fully supported. The only requirement is that SAP Cloud Identity Services is the first common trust anchor in the chain – meaning all participating web applications are configured to trust it directly, even if it in turn proxies to a corporate IdP.

One configuration detail to be aware of in proxy setups: the "Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP" setting in SAP Cloud Identity Services must not be enabled for the applications involved in the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow. When this setting is active, SAP Cloud Identity Services bypasses its own session handling and always redirects to the corporate IdP – which would cause the Mobile-to-Web SSO token to be ignored, and users would end up with a login prompt again. Beyond Mobile-to-Web SSO, enabling this setting can also cause issues in iFrame-based in-place app integration scenarios, as corporate identity providers frequently block iFraming to protect against clickjacking and similar attack vectors. For more information on this setting, see Forward All SSO Requests to Corporate IdP (SAP Help).

The trust for the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow is configured explicitly in SAP Cloud Identity Services. The administrator defines which web application entries in SAP Cloud Identity Services are allowed to participate in this flow – only applications that have been explicitly added to the trust configuration can benefit from it. In addition, SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the URL of the target web application against the allowed redirect URIs configured for the trusted application. This means a web application can only receive a seamless SSO session if its URL matches one of the permitted redirect URI patterns. Both aspects – the application trust list and the redirect URI validation – ensure that the administrator retains full control over which applications can be reached via this mechanism.

Please note: Customers with SAP Build Work Zone subscriptions created before March 2025 may not yet be using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication directly. For these environments, it is necessary to explicitly switch the authentication mechanism. Subscriptions created after that date are automatically set up with SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. Please refer to the SAP Help documentation for the migration steps: Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.

Pro tip: As with other features that depend on shared sessions and cookies – such as the iFrame-based in-place integration of web applications – it is important that all components share a common super domain. We strongly recommend operating SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication and your SAP cloud solutions under the SAP Common Super Domain cloud.sap. If you are not yet familiar with this topic, our earlier blog post explains the background and the configuration steps in detail: Demystifying the Common Super Domain for SAP Mobile Start.

Which Web Applications Are Covered Automatically?

When Joule Work mobile app is connected to SAP Build Work Zone, both share the same Application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication. This means that SAP Build Work Zone itself is automatically within the trust scope – no additional configuration is needed for it.

This automatic coverage includes for example:

  • SAP Build Work Zone, advanced edition Workspaces – collaborative workspace content embedded in the Work Zone experience
  • Custom extensions hosted on SAP BTP – applications built for example with the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, deployed to the SAP BTP and served using the Approuter of SAP Build Work Zone. (For details on building such extensions, see Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start)
  • SAP Fiori apps from SAP S/4HANA On-Premise or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition running through an SAP Cloud Connector using a Principal Propagation Destination – these are tunneled through the BTP subaccount and are therefore covered by the same Application entry

The key distinction is that automatic coverage applies to applications that are part of – or tunneled through – the SAP BTP subaccount and its corresponding SAP Cloud Identity Services Application. Applications that run on their own infrastructure and have their own independent trust relationship with SAP Cloud Identity Services are not automatically covered, regardless of whether they are launched in embedded ("in-place") mode or opened directly ("ex-place"). A prominent example is SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: even when it is iFramed within the SAP Build Work Zone shell, it has its own Application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services and must be explicitly added to the Native-Web Trust group.

For all such web applications – SAP solutions with their own IAS Application entry, third-party apps, or any app not part of the Work Zone BTP subaccount – administrators add them to the trust configuration in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as described in the setup steps below.

Administrator Setup

Step 1: Verify That IAS-Based Authentication Is Active

Before enabling the feature, confirm that SAP Build Work Zone is using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication as its identity provider. If this is not yet the case, follow the migration steps referenced in the prerequisite section above.

Step 2: Enable the Feature Flag in SAP Mobile Services

In the SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit, navigate to the Client Settings. In the Feature Flags section, locate the entry named "MobiletoWebSingleSign-On" and activate it.

Screenshot showing the highlighted feature flag in the activated state in the Mobile Services Admin Cockpit.

For reference, the corresponding documentation can be found here: Single Sign-On for Web Apps – SAP Help

Step 3: Add Trusted Web Applications in SAP Cloud Identity Services

For web applications that are not automatically covered through the SAP Build Work Zone application (see the previous section), you can add them to the trust configuration:

  1. Sign in to the administration console for SAP Cloud Identity Services.
  2. Go to Applications & Resources → Applications.
  3. Select the application entry that corresponds to your SAP Build Work Zone instance.
  4. Go to the Trust tab, then navigate to Application APIs → Dependencies → Native-Web Trust.
  5. Click Add and select the application with which you want to establish trust.
  6. Save your selection.

Screenshot of SAP Cloud Identity Services to maintain Native-Web Trust Settings

Users opening applications from this trust list in the in-app browser will now benefit from the seamless Mobile-to-Web SSO flow.

Step 4: Verify the Redirect URIs of Trusted Applications

As described in the prerequisite section, SAP Cloud Identity Services validates the URL of the target web application against the allowed redirect URIs of the trusted application entry. For SAP-delivered solutions – such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – the redirect URIs are typically pre-configured correctly by SAP and no further action is needed.

However, for custom-built applications or third-party web apps that rely on the general SAP Business Technology Platform application entry in SAP Cloud Identity Services, you should verify that the application's URL is covered by the configured redirect URI patterns. If the URL does not match any permitted pattern, the Mobile-to-Web SSO flow will not complete successfully for that application.

You can review and adjust the redirect URIs in the SAP Cloud Identity Services administration console under the application's configuration. For the rules and wildcard syntax supported for redirect URIs, refer to the SAP Help documentation: Redirect URIs and Post Logout Redirect URI Rules (SAP Help).

Summary

With the Mobile-to-Web SSO feature, Joule Work mobile app version 3.0 closes the session gap between the native app context and the in-app browser – cleanly, securely, and with full administrator control.

Key takeaways:

  • The feature eliminates the need for long-lived "Remember me" cookies on the identity provider, which are a security concern and not universally available.
  • It is based on short-lived, one-time use SSO tokens issued and validated by SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication, which acts as the common trust anchor.
  • The feature is optional and in control by the customer: an administrator (de-/) activates it via a feature flag in SAP Mobile Services Admin Cockpit of Joule Work mobile app and controls which web applications participate via the Native-Web Trust configuration in SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication.
  • SAP Build Work Zone web UIs and tunneled embedded business apps are automatically covered. Additional apps can be added with a few clicks.
  • The feature is compatible with all in-app browser variants in Joule Work mobile app and all trusted web applications, regardless of whether they are SAP solutions or custom apps.

If you need further assistance configuring and making use of this new feature in the Joule Work mobile app, feel free to contact our product management team using jouleworkmobile@sap.com, which will be able to suggest the next steps for your scenario.

Further Reading

  • Joule Work mobile app – Single Sign-On for Web Apps (SAP Help)
  • Switching to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (SAP Help)
  • Demystifying the Common Super Domain for SAP Mobile Start (SAP Community Blog)
  • Building Pro-Code Extensions for SAP Mobile Start – Leveraging Mobile Native Features (SAP Community Blog)
  • SAP Mobile Experience Community Page

entry.title: Re: Role Creation and Restriction Consolidation Unit wise...
entry.content:
Good day @M_Mahajan 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, the issue is not solved by you assigning SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_BVREAD_PC or SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_PROCESS_PC without restrictions. That only hides the authorisation error by giving the user wider access than intended.

The correct approach is for you to give the user the required Group Reporting processing and read access but maintain the same restriction values consistently across the relevant Group Reporting business catalogs.

Here are references for more guiding details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/705cf2cb6fbd41469f8fdb8ed26f8dfd.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/90c07e91c7a64f328be3fd6b48955b13/7f023b8f3d00435b8973c5ba49b46e9f.html?locale=en-US

So, in simple terms:

  • Currency Translation and Consolidation Data Validation need more than only the visible Data Monitor or Consolidation Monitor tile access.
  • These tasks also need access to Group Reporting processing during execution.
  • The error you received means the user is missing authorisation to read the selection object used by the task.
  • Giving SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_BVREAD_PC or SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_PROCESS_PC unrestricted fixes the error technically but it also gives too much access.
  • The better approach is to include the required catalogs but restrict them consistently by the same consolidation group or consolidation unit values.
  • Do not mix one restricted Group Reporting role with another unrestricted Group Reporting role for the same user. The reason is, the unrestricted role can in practice override the intended segregation.

The role setup I recommend is as follows:

  1. Use Maintain Business Roles app to create a dedicated restricted Group Reporting processing role. Include the business catalogs you need for the user’s task.  Since your test shows that SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_BVREAD_PC or SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_PROCESS_PC is required for the task to execute, these catalogs should not simply be added unrestricted. They should be added to the same restricted role and restricted with the same consolidation unit or consolidation group scope.                                                                                                                                                         
  2. In Maintain Restrictions, maintain the same restriction values across all Group Reporting catalogs. Typically, you check the consolidation version, fiscal year or period, consolidation group, ledger or other reporting dimensions, etc. The important part is that the restriction should not only be maintained for the Data Monitor catalog. It must also be maintained for the read and process catalogs that the task uses in the background.  This is why your unrestricted role test worked. The missing authorisation is most likely in the background access needed by the task, not only in the app tile itself.
  3. Avoid assigning an additional unrestricted Group Reporting role with SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_BVREAD_PC or SAP_FIN_BC_CCON_PROCESS_PC unrestricted to the same user. That design technically enables the tasks but it defeats the restriction by allowing access to all consolidation units or groups. Instead of that, either:
    1. Add the missing catalog to the existing restricted Group Reporting role and maintain the same restrictions, or
    2. Create a separate support role with those catalogs but maintain the same consolidation unit or group restrictions there as well.
  4. Check whether the selection object itself needs read or value help access. The error mentioning selection class CS_SEL points to missing access to selection definitions used by the Currency Translation or validation task. Currency Translation and validation task may use predefined selection objects, methods, or task settings. If the user cannot display the required selection object, the task cannot resolve the selection and fails. Therefore, when maintaining restrictions, also check the access category for Read access, Value help access and Selection object display access, if available in the role restriction UI.
  5. If you add the required catalogs with the same consolidation unit or group restrictions and the task still fails with CS_SEL, then i suggest you raise a case.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: E-Invoicing for self billing in France E-Invoice
entry.content: To move forward, you should conduct a gap analysis on MRRL invoice flows, then develop a custom mapping to generate Factur‑X XML from MRRL self‑invoices, and integrate this into SAP DRC for transmission to your chosen PA/PDP. I recommend engaging an SAP compliance partner to help design and pilot the MRRL → DRC → PA integration flow, ensuring monitoring and exception handling are in place before rollout.

entry.title: SII tip for Spain #12: Defer Taxes: VAT on Cash (RECC)
entry.content:
SII requires Spain-based companies to report not just invoices, but also the payments linked to suppliers registered under the VAT on Cash scheme (RECC — Régimen Especial del Criterio de Caja). If your company is not under RECC but buys from suppliers that are, you have specific SII obligations that go beyond the standard invoice submission — and getting them wrong means your VAT deduction timing will be off. In this post we'll cover exactly what SII expects in this scenario and how to configure S/4HANA to handle it automatically, from the vendor master to the daily RFUMSV50 run (Remark use App "Deferred Tax Transfer" in case of S/4HANA Cloud Public edition).

 

Introduction

The Special Regime for VAT on Cash (RECC — Régimen Especial del Criterio de Caja), regulated in articles 163 decies to 163 sexiesdecies of the Spanish VAT Act, defers VAT accrual until the actual collection or payment of the invoice. Its impact on SII extends beyond the companies that apply the regime: any company receiving invoices from a supplier registered under RECC has specific obligations to record payments in the Received Invoices Register Book.

In S/4HANA, the process combines specific customizing for deferred tax codes, the periodic execution of program RFUMSV50 (App "Deferred Tax Transfer"), and the creation of SII eDocuments for payments. If any of these steps is missing or misconfigured, the SII entries will be incorrect — either because the payment is never reported, or because the wrong special regime key is used.

 

Business Scenario

RECC is designed for small businesses and self-employed individuals with a turnover below 2.000.000 € in the previous calendar year (art. 163 decies VAT Act). Companies running SAP systems rarely fall within this threshold, so the most common scenario is not a company applying RECC itself, but a company purchasing from a supplier that does.

In that case, by applying art. 163 quindecies of the VAT Act to SII,  the recipient shall:

  • Record the received invoice with special regime key "07" in the Received Invoices Register Book.
  • Record each payment in the same book, stating the date, amount, and payment method.
  • Deduct the input VAT in the period when the payment is made — not in the period when the invoice was received.

The most affected sectors are retail distribution and professional services, where it is common to deal with smaller suppliers registered under RECC.

 

How RECC Works in SII

The XML tag <ClaveRegimenEspecialOTrascendencia> must be 07 on the received invoice. The field <Medio> in the payment is derived from the payment method of the FI document (BSEG-ZLSCH) mapped against table T042Z:

 

XML valuePayment method
01Bank transfer
02Cheque
03Not paid
04Other methods
05Direct debit

Note: If the standard derivation does not cover your business case, BAdI FIESSII_PAYMENT_METHOD allows you to override the <Medio> value per document.

 

Configuration in S/4HANA (company NOT under RECC)

The following configuration is required when your company is not under RECC but has suppliers that are.

 

1. Register the supplier as a RECC supplier

In the vendor master, activate the "VoC supplier" flag. This controls that invoices received from that vendor automatically use deferred tax codes.

 

2. Configure deferred tax codes — FIESSII_TAXCODEV (SSCUI 102558)

Define the mapping between the original tax code and the deferred target tax code:

Examples:

Source codeDescriptionTarget code (deferred)
IZInput VAT 21 % (RECC)V2
IRInput VAT 10 % (RECC)V1
ISInput VAT 4 % (RECC)V0

 

 

3. Configure FI document type for the transfer posting — FIESSII_DOCTYPEV  (SSCUI 102557)

The transfer posting documents generated by RFUMSV50 (App "Deferred Tax Transfer") must be classified as type "Z2" in table  FIESSII_DOCTYPEV.  Without this configuration, the defer tax program will not find the document type and will not generate the SII payment eDocument.

Note: The document type "Z2" is an SAP-specific identifier only for this particular use case and is not defined by the Tax Agency (AEAT). It is valid here since the SuministroLRPagosRecibidas web service does not include an invoice type field — so the AEAT never sees the "Z2" value. If a document with type "Z2" were submitted through SuministroLRFacturasRecibidas or SuministroLRFacturasEmitidas (both of which require a valid AEAT invoice type), the message would be rejected by the Tax Agency.

 

4. Assign GL accounts to deferred tax codes — SSCUI 103803

In "Assign General Ledger Accounts to Tax Codes" assign the deferred VAT GL accounts that RFUMSV50 (App "Deferred Tax Transfer")  will use when posting the transfer. Accounts must be configured both for the pending deduction position (suspense account) and for the final deductible VAT account.

 

Operational Process

  • Post the received invoice with the RECC tax code (e.g. IZ). The system records VAT in the deferred tax account, with no immediate deduction.

  • Create the SII eDocument for the received invoice using ESSII_EDOCCREATE + ESSII_EDOCLIST.  It will be sent with regime key 07 to the SII Received Invoices Register Book.

              

  • Run the payment using transaction F-53  (or automatic payment run F110) when agreed with the supplier.

  • Run the defer taxes program daily: RFUMSV50 (App "Deferred Tax Transfer") .  This program identifies payment documents pending transfer and generates the accounting entries that move VAT from the deferred account to the deductible VAT account.

    ⚠️ Important: The Reference field (XBLNR) on the transfer posting document is the link to the original invoice. Do not modify it manually — the SII process uses it to build the payment eDocument.

     

  • Create the SII eDocument for the payment using ESSII_EDOCCREATE +  ESSII_EDOCLIST.  This triggers the submission to web service SuministroLRPagosRecibidas with the payment date, amount, and payment method (<Medio>).

 

           

 

Practical Example

Scenario: a customer company (not under RECC) receives on 10/03/2025 an invoice from a RECC-registered supplier for a total of 12,100 € (10,000 € net + 2,100 € VAT at 21%). Payment is made on 15/04/2025 by bank transfer.

 

StepDateSAP action SII outcome
1. Invoice receipt10/03/2025Posted with code IZ → VAT held in deferred account

Sent to SuministroLRFacturasRecibidas, key 07, no deductible VAT yet

2. Payment15/04/2025F-53 payment method "T" (transfer, mapped to code 01 in T042Z)Payment document generated
3. VAT transfer16/04/2025Overnight RFUMSV50: moves 2,100 € from deferred to deductible VAT accountTransfer posting with XBLNR = original invoice number
4. Payment eDoc16/04/2025ESSII_EDOCCREATE / ESSII_EDOCLISTSent to SuministroLRPagosRecibidas: date 15/04/2025, amount 12,100 €, method 01

The 2,100 € VAT is deductible in period 04/2025 (payment date), not in period 03/2025 (invoice receipt date).

 

Additional Considerations

  • RECC exceptions: the following transactions cannot be included under RECC even if the taxpayer is registered in the regime: intra-community transactions, transactions with recipients not established in Spain (TAI), and reverse-charge transactions. These invoices must be reported with the standard regime keys.

  • SII deadline for RECC payments: according to the AEAT FAQ (v1.1, Feb. 2026), collections and payments of RECC transactions must be reported within the general deadline of 4 business days from the transaction date (Saturdays, Sundays and national public holidays excluded).

  • Key 07 always in first position: when an invoice combines key 07 (RECC) with another special regime key (e.g. 01 general regime), key 07 must always be declared first in the <ClaveRegimenEspecialOTrascendencia> tag.

  • Do not alter XBLNR : the reference field on the transfer posting document generated by  RFUMSV50 automatically links the payment to the original invoice. Any manual modification breaks this link and the payment eDocument will be sent without the correct invoice reference.

 

Further Reading

In case of additional questions, you can visit the SII help documentation in the following links:

  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Public Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in S/4HANA Private Cloud
  • Electronic Tax Register Books with SII in ERP
  • Other SII tips — Spain SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Community

Disclaimer

This blog post reflects the author's interpretation of Spanish legal requirements and SAP system behavior as of the publication date. It is intended for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. For specific guidance, consult your legal or tax advisor and refer to official SAP documentation. The links to external sources in this blog may be modified at any time externally by the owner without previous notice.

entry.title: Re: SAP Asset Performance Management Embedding Cumulocity to Drive Innovations in IoT and AI
entry.content:
@daniel_brecht, not sure what your question is about but by default time series data reaching devices in embedded Cumulocity can be mapped using the "Digital Twin Manager" in embedded Cumulocity to assets and their indicators in APM. If you check our some of the subsequent blogs from the likes of @florian_weigold or @EricSander this is described in more detail there.

If you talk about integrating Cumulocity as a standalone offering (purchased from SAP Store or from Cumulocity directly) then there are many ingestions paths that each have their pros and cons but for the most part this integration has to be built in the project:

  1. APIs in general from ERP and APM to replicate master data into the SAP world. On the ERP side there are many middleware tools and the transport system that can be used.
  2. File upload api for bulk upload of time series data into APM.
  3. Ingestion via MQTT, REST or other common protocols into Cumulocity with or without leveraging thing edge.
  4. Creation of measurement documents via api in ERP or individual measurements in APM via api - this only makes sense for a very small number of measurements (e.g. counter reading).

entry.title: Re: In Manage Production Orders, the column “Order Text”...
entry.content:
Good day @Jana_Hauck 

Thank you for your question.

The Order Text column in Manage Production orders is not filled from production order header long text, the operation long text, or other maintained long text section. It refers to the production order short description/order text field exposed as ManufacturingOrderText.

If the Order Text column is empty, check whether the short order description/order text is maintained on the production order. Maintaining only header long text or order long text will not automatically populate this list column. Note that manufacturing order long text is a separate text field from ManufacturingOrderText. Basically, saying that they are separate data sources.

See the following references for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/8a8c0ffa65b14ca0aaf9678ed5b2b44c.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/3df782bd3a824e948825154000602c53.html?locale=en-US

For capacity planning, there is no standard setting to show production order long text or shift notes directly as planning table columns. You can create shift notes with reference to objects such as production orders, but they are handled through the shift note function are not automatically displayed as order text in capacity planning apps.

I suggest you test one production order by maintaining the short order description, refreshing Manage Production Orders and checking whether the Order Text column is filled.  

Then test the same order in the capacity planning app and check the available column settings. 

 

 If your business requires production order long text or shift notes directly in the planning table, this should be raised as an improvement request or handled through a custom reporting approach using released CDS views where available.

Here is more reference for more guidance

3491464 - Element which exists in the underlying custom CDS view is not selectable in Fields Selections of custom analytical query - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Mass Upload of Equipment Classification & Characteristics Data Using LTMC in SAP
entry.content:
During one of my SAP PM data migration activities, I had a requirement to upload thousands of equipment classification records into SAP. The equipment masters were already available in the system, but the challenge was assigning the correct class and characteristic values to each equipment in bulk. Initially, I thought of maintaining it manually by LSMW in IE02, but it quickly became difficult because of the huge volume of records. That is when I explored the standard LTMC template available in SAP S/4HANA and found it more efficient and reliable approach for this requirement.

In this blog, I would like to share how we used LTMC to upload equipment class and characteristic values, and a few important points that helped me complete the migration successfully.

USE CASE OVERVIEW

For this requirement, I had to upload classification data for Fleet Equipment records that were already created in SAP. Each equipment needed to be assigned to the class, and characteristics are assigned to it.

The Characteristics are:

  1. Capacity
  2. Power

Let us take the sample record used during the upload below.

Equipment

Class Type

Class

Characteristic1

Value

Characteristic2

Value

Heavy Vehicle1

002

Tipper Truck

Capacity

30 ton

Power

220kW

Heavy Vehicle2

002

Tractor

Capacity

40 ton

Power

300kW

Heavy Vehicle3

002

Crane

Capacity

120 ton

Power

350 kW

PREREQUISITES:

Before starting the upload through Migration Cockpit, a few master data and configuration activities must already be completed in the system. Ensuring these prerequisites beforehand helped avoid most of the validation errors during the upload process. Below are the main prerequisites required for uploading equipment classification data using LTMC.

  1. Equipment Master
  2. Characteristics Created
  3. Class Created

Note: Correct class type should be used. In this example, Class Type 002 was used for Equipment Classification.

MIGRATION COCKPIT PROCESS

Step :1 - Open Migration Cockpit

  • Open the Migration Cockpit
  • Create a new project by providing the project name
  • After creating the project, select the migration object: “Object Classification- General template”

SAP provides a standard migration template for Classification, since the equipment masters were already available in the system

Step: 2 – Download the Template

Download the standard Excel template provided by SAP. The template contains multiple sheets. For this activity, the important sheets were related to:

  • Equipment data
  • Class assignment
  • Characteristic values

One thing I noticed during preparation was that the characteristic names in Excel must exactly match the characteristic names maintained in SAP. Otherwise, validation errors occur during upload.

Step:3 - Maintain Classification Data

There are two key areas to fill in:

i) Header Tab: Enter the equipment number along with the assigned class type and class name for each record.

ii) Numerical Value Allocation:  Enter the characteristic names and their corresponding values for each equipment. For numerical characteristics such as Capacity and Power, ensure the values are entered in the correct format as defined in the SAP characteristic configuration before proceeding to the upload step.

Step -4: Upload the File and Execute Migration

Once the Excel file was prepared, upload the file into LTMC. SAP reads the uploaded data and performs initial checks. The following five steps in the LTMC has to be followed,

Step

Action

Details

1

Upload

Upload the prepared Excel file into the Migration Cockpit.

2

Prepare

SAP reads and validates the uploaded data against system configuration.

3

Map Fields

Map the source Excel columns to the corresponding SAP target fields.

4

Simulate

Run a simulation to identify errors before actual migration.

5

Migrate

Execute the final migration to post classification data into SAP.

These Steps are particularly important as it identifies issues such as:

  • Missing classes
  • Invalid characteristics
  • Incorrect data formats
  • Equipment number issues

Note: Always test with a small batch of records first before uploading large volumes of data. This makes troubleshooting much easier.

POST MIGRATION VERIFICATION

Once the migration was completed successfully, the equipment classification data was updated automatically in SAP. After the upload, I verified the results using the transaction IE03. Under the Classification tab, the class assignment and characteristic values were updated correctly for the equipment. This confirmed that the migration was completed as expected.

CONCLUSION

Using the standard LTMC template made the equipment classification upload process much easier compared to maintaining the data manually through IE02 or LSMW. The most important learning for me during this activity was that proper preparation of Excel data plays a major role in successful migration. Even small inconsistencies in characteristic names or data types can cause upload failures. I hope this example helps anyone working on SAP PM data migration or equipment classification upload activities in SAP S/4HANA.
